subject: Mathematics
q: The set of all real values of $\lambda$ for which exactly two common tangents can be drawn to the circles $x^2+y^2-4 x-4 y+6=0$ and $\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2-10 \mathrm{x}-10 \mathrm{y}+\lambda=0$ is the interval:
question: The set of all real values of $\lambda$ for which exactly two common tangents can be drawn to the circles $x^2+y^2-4 x-4 y+6=0$ and $\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2-10 \mathrm{x}-10 \mathrm{y}+\lambda=0$ is the interval:
solution:
The equations of the circles are
$$
x^2+y^2-10 x-10 y+\lambda=0
$$
and $x^2+y^2-4 x-4 y+6=0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&C_1=\text { centre of }(1)=(5,5) \\
&\mathrm{C}_2=\text { centre of }(2)=(2,2) \\
&d=\text { distance between centres } \\
&=\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2=\sqrt{9+9}=\sqrt{18} \\
&r_1=\sqrt{50-\lambda}, r_2=\sqrt{2} \\
&\text { For exactly two common tangents we } \\
&\text { have } \\
&r_1-r_2 < C_1 C_2 < r_1+r_2 \\
&\Rightarrow \sqrt{50-\lambda}-\sqrt{2} < 3 \sqrt{2} < \sqrt{50-\lambda}+\sqrt{2} \\
&\Rightarrow \sqrt{50-\lambda}-\sqrt{2} < 3 \sqrt{2} \text { or } 3 \sqrt{2} < \sqrt{50-\lambda}+\sqrt{2} \\
&\Rightarrow \sqrt{50-\lambda} < 4 \sqrt{2} \text { or } 2 \sqrt{2} < \sqrt{50-\lambda} \\
&\Rightarrow 50-\lambda < 32 \text { or } 8 < 50-\lambda \\
&\Rightarrow \lambda>18 \text { or } \lambda < 42 \\
&\text { Required interval is }(18,42) \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: A circle cuts a chord of length 4 a on the $x$ -axis and passes through a point on the $y$ -axis, distant $2 \mathrm{~b}$ from the origin. Then the locus of the centre of this circle, is:
question: A circle cuts a chord of length 4 a on the $x$ -axis and passes through a point on the $y$ -axis, distant $2 \mathrm{~b}$ from the origin. Then the locus of the centre of this circle, is:
solution: Let centre be $C(h, k)$ $C Q=C P=r$ $\Rightarrow C Q^{2}=C P^{2}$ $(h-0)^{2}+(k \pm 0)^{2}=C M^{2}+M P^{2}$ $h^{2}+(k \pm 2 \mathrm{~b})^{2}=k^{2}+4 a^{2}$ $h^{2}+k^{2}+4 b^{2} \pm 4 b k=k^{2}+4 a^{2}$ Then, the locus of centre $C(h, k)$ $x^{2}+4 b^{2} \pm 4 b y=4 a^{2}$ Hence, the above locus of the centre of circle is a parabola.

subject: Mathematics
q:
A circle touches the $x$-axis and also touches the circle with centre at $(0,3)$ and radius
2. The locus of the centre of the circle is
question:
A circle touches the $x$-axis and also touches the circle with centre at $(0,3)$ and radius
2. The locus of the centre of the circle is
solution: Equation of circle with centre $(0,3)$ and radius 2 is $x^2+(y-3)^2=4$ Let locus of the variable circle is $(\alpha, \beta)$ $\because$ It touches $x$-axis. $\therefore$ It equation $(x-\alpha)^2+(y-\beta)^2=\beta^2$ Circles touch externally $$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \sqrt{\alpha^2+(\beta-3)^2}=2+\beta \\ & \alpha^2+(\beta-3)^2=\beta^2+4+4 \beta \\ & \alpha^2=10(\beta-1 / 2) \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ Locus is $x^2=10(y-1 / 2)$ which is parabola.

 ∴ Centre is 3, 5 and radius is 34-p.

q: Consider a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the circle touches the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math>, whose distance from the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _______.
question: Consider a circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the circle touches the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math>, whose distance from the origin is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></math> , then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _______.
solution:
Given: x-α2+y-β2=50

The given equation of circle represent centre as α,β and radius as 52 units.

Now, x+y=0 is tangent to the given circle at P.

We know that, radius is perpendicular to tangent at the point of tangency.

⇒CP⊥x+y=0 and CP=r

⇒r=α×1+β×112+12

⇒52=α+β2

⇒50=α+β22

⇒α+β2=100

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the arc $A C$ of a circle subtend a right angle at the centre $O$. If the point $B$ on the arc $A C$, divides the arc $A C$ such that $\frac{\text { length of } \operatorname{arc} A B}{\text { length of } \operatorname{arc} B C}=\frac{1}{5}$, and $\overrightarrow{O C}=\alpha \overrightarrow{O A}+\beta \overrightarrow{O B}$, then $\alpha+\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{3}-1) \beta$ is equal to
question: Let the arc $A C$ of a circle subtend a right angle at the centre $O$. If the point $B$ on the arc $A C$, divides the arc $A C$ such that $\frac{\text { length of } \operatorname{arc} A B}{\text { length of } \operatorname{arc} B C}=\frac{1}{5}$, and $\overrightarrow{O C}=\alpha \overrightarrow{O A}+\beta \overrightarrow{O B}$, then $\alpha+\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{3}-1) \beta$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\alpha \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\beta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \ldots . .(1) \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\alpha \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\beta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \\ & 0=\alpha+\beta \cos 15^{\circ} \ldots .(2) \\ & (1) \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\alpha \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+\beta \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos 75^{\circ}=\alpha \cos 15^{\circ}+\beta \ldots .(3) \\ & (2) \&(3) \Rightarrow \cos 75^{\circ}=-\beta \cos ^2 15^{\circ}+\beta\end{aligned}$ $\beta=\frac{\cos 75^{\circ}}{\sin ^2 15^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{\sin 15^{\circ}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}-1}$ (2) $\Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{-\cos 15^{\circ}}{\sin 15^{\circ}}=\frac{-(\sqrt{3}+1)}{(\sqrt{3}-1)}$ $\therefore \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=\frac{-(\sqrt{3}+1)}{(\sqrt{3}-1)} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+\left(\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}-1}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}$ Now $\begin{aligned} & \alpha+\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{3}-1) \beta=\frac{-(\sqrt{3}+1)}{(\sqrt{3}-1)}+\frac{\sqrt{2}(\sqrt{3}-1) \cdot 2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}-1} \\ & =\frac{-(\sqrt{3}+1)^2}{2}+4 \\ & =\frac{-3-1-2 \sqrt{3}+8}{2} \\ & =2-\sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$

x2+y2-22x-10y+137=0
options: circles have only one meeting point
solution:
x2+y2-10x-10y+41=0

A(5,5),R1=3

B(11,5),R2=3

AB=6=R1+R2

Touch each other externally

⇒ circles have only one meeting point.

question: If a line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math>, is a tangent to the circle <math><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, and it is perpendicular to a line <math><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> where <math><msub><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is the tangent to the circle <math><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
solution:
Slope of tangent to x2+y2=1 at 12,12
x2+y2=1
2x+2yy'=0
y'=-xy=-1
Given that, y=mx+c, is perpendicular to L1,
So, m=1 ⇒y=x+c

Now distance of 3,0 from y=x+c is
c+32=1
c2+6c+9=2
c2+6c+7=0.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the centre of a circle, passing through the points $(0,0),(1,0)$ and touching the circle $x^2+y^2=9$, be $(h, k)$. Then for all possible values of the coordinates of the centre $(h, k), 4\left(h^2+k^2\right)$ is equal to_________
question: Let the centre of a circle, passing through the points $(0,0),(1,0)$ and touching the circle $x^2+y^2=9$, be $(h, k)$. Then for all possible values of the coordinates of the centre $(h, k), 4\left(h^2+k^2\right)$ is equal to_________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{h})^2+(\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{k})^2=\mathrm{h}^2+\mathrm{k}^2 \\ & \mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{y}^2-2 \mathrm{hx}-2 \mathrm{ky}=0 \\ & \because \text { passes through }(1,0) \\ & \Rightarrow 1+0-2 \mathrm{~h}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}=1 / 2 \\ & \because \mathrm{OC}=\frac{\mathrm{OP}}{2} \\ & \sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\mathrm{k}^2}=\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{4}+\mathrm{k}^2=\frac{9}{4} \\ & \mathrm{k}^2=2 \\ & \mathrm{k}= \pm \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Possible coordinate of $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{c}(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{k})\left(\frac{1}{2}, \sqrt{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2},-\sqrt{2}\right) \\ & 4\left(\mathrm{~h}^2+\mathrm{k}^2\right)=4\left(\frac{1}{4}+2\right)=4\left(\frac{9}{4}\right)=9 \end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Locus of mid point of the portion between the axes of $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p$ where $p$ is constant is
question: Locus of mid point of the portion between the axes of $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p$ where $p$ is constant is
solution: Equation of $A B$ is $x \cos \alpha+y \sin \alpha=p \Rightarrow \frac{x \cos \alpha}{p}+\frac{y \sin \alpha}{p}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{p / \cos \alpha}+\frac{y}{p / \sin \alpha}=1$ So co-ordinates of $A$ and $B$ are $\left(\frac{p}{\cos \alpha}, 0\right)$ and $\left(0, \frac{p}{\sin \alpha}\right)$; So coordinates of mid point of $A B$ are $\left(\frac{p}{2 \cos \alpha}, \frac{p}{2 \sin \alpha}\right)=\left(x_1, y_1\right)($ let $) ; x_1=\frac{p}{2 \cos \alpha} \& y_1=\frac{p}{2 \sin \alpha}$ $\Rightarrow \cos \alpha=p / 2 x_1$ and $\sin \alpha=p / 2 y_1 ; \cos ^2 \alpha+\sin ^2 \alpha=1 \Rightarrow \frac{p^2}{4}\left(\frac{1}{x_1^2}+\frac{1}{y_1^2}\right)=1$ Locus of $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is $\frac{1}{x^2}+\frac{1}{y^2}=\frac{4}{p^2}$.

q: A circle passes through the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. If its centre lies on the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then its radius is equal to :
question: A circle passes through the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. solution:
Let centre of circle be Cα, β.

It lies in line y-4x+3-0⇒β=4α-3

∴ C α, 4α-3

∴CA=CB

By distance formula,

⇒α-22+4α-62=α-42+4α-82

⇒-4α+4-48α+36=-8α+16-64α+64

⇒64+8-4-48α=80-40

⇒α=4020=2

∴ C2,5

∴ r=2-22+5-32=2

q: Two tangents are drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If these tangents touch the circle at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is a point on the circle such that length of the segments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are equal, then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is equal to:
question: Two tangents are drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If these tangents touch the circle at points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> is a point on the circle such that length of the segments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are equal, then the area of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given, the equation of circle is x2+y2-2x-6y+6=0

So, the centre of the circle is 1,3 and the radius is 12+32-6=2

Assume, the coordinate of point B is -1,k and the coordinate of point A is h,1.

So, h+12=2⇒h=1 and k-12=2⇒k=3

So, the coordinate of B is -1,3 and the coordinate of A is 1,1.

From the diagram, area of ∆ABD=12×2×4

=4 sq. units

subject: Mathematics
q: If a variable line <math><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is such that the two circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>78</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are on its opposite sides, then the set of all values of <math><mi>λ</mi></math> is the interval :
question: If a variable line <math><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is such that the two circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>18</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>78</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are on its opposite sides, then the set of all values of <math><mi>λ</mi></math> is the interval :
solution:
Given circles x2+y2-2x-2y+1=0⇒x-12+y-12=12 and

x2+y2-18x-2y+78=0⇒x-92+y-12=22

Thus, centers of two circles are 1, 1 & 9, 1 and radii are 1 & 2 respectively.

Since, center of circles are on the opposite side of line 3x+4y-λ=0.

⇒3+4-λ27+4-λ<0

⇒7-λ31-λ<0

⇒λ∈7, 31 …….i

Since, circles should not intersect given line.

Hence, 7-λ32+42≥1   &   31-λ32+42≥2

⇒7-λ≥5  & 31-λ≥10

⇒λ≤2 or  λ≥12 …….ii

and λ≤21 or λ≥41 …….iii

Thus, λ∈i∩ii∩iii

⇒λ∈12, 21

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the locus of the centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, of the circle which touches the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> externally and also touches the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. Then the area bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is
question: Let the locus of the centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>, of the circle which touches the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> externally and also touches the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. Then the area bounded by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is
solution:
Since the circle with centre α,β touches the circle x2+y-12=1 externally and also touches x-axis,

therefore the distance between the centres will be equal to the sum of the radius of both circles

i.e. α-02+β-12=β+1

⇒α2=4β

Hence the locus of L will be x2=4y

Now, the area bounded by x2=4y & y=4 will be

A=2∫044-x24dx

=24x-x31204=643

subject: Mathematics
q: If the pair of lines $a x^2+2(a+b) x y+b y^2=0$ lie along diameters of a circle and divide the circle into four sectors such that the area of one of the sectors is thrice the area of another sector then
question: If the pair of lines $a x^2+2(a+b) x y+b y^2=0$ lie along diameters of a circle and divide the circle into four sectors such that the area of one of the sectors is thrice the area of another sector then
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left|\frac{2 \sqrt{(a+b)^2-a b}}{a+b}\right|=1 \\
& \Rightarrow(a+b)^2=4\left(a^2+b^2+a b\right) \\
& \Rightarrow 3 a^2+3 b^2+2 a b=0 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let

 A=x,y∈R×R∣2x2+2y2-2x-2y=1

B=x,y∈R×R∣4x2+4y2-16y+7=0 and

C=x,y∈R×R∣x2+y2-4x-2y+5≤r2. Then the minimum value of r such that A∪B⊆C is equal to
question:
Let

 A=x,y∈R×R∣2x2+2y2-2x-2y=1

B=x,y∈R×R∣4x2+4y2-16y+7=0 and

C=x,y∈R×R∣x2+y2-4x-2y+5≤r2. Then the minimum value of r such that A∪B⊆C is equal to
solution:
Given,

S1:x2+y2-x-y-12=0

So, the centre is C1:12, 12 and the radius is r1=14+14+12=1

S2:x2+y2-4y+74=0

So, the centre is C2:0,2 and the radius is 

r2=4-74=32

S3:x2+y2-4x-2y+5-r2=0

So, the centre is C3:2,1 and the radius is 

r3=4+1-5+r2=r

From the diagram, we can say that if A∪B⊆C, then the circle A & B should lie in the circle C

Here, C1C3=52

So, 52≤r-1⇒r≤1-52r≥1+52

Also, C2C3=5≤r-32

⇒r≥5+32r≤32-5

So, the minimum value of r=3+252

subject: Mathematics
q: If the line $y=m x+1$ meets the circle $x^2+y^2+3 x=0$ in two points equidistant from and on opposite sides of $x$-axis, then
question: If the line $y=m x+1$ meets the circle $x^2+y^2+3 x=0$ in two points equidistant from and on opposite sides of $x$-axis, then
solution:
Circle: $x^2+y^2+3 x=0$ Centre, $B=\left(-\frac{3}{2}, 0\right)$
$y$-intercept of the line $=1$ $$ \therefore \quad \mathrm{A}=(0,1) $$ Slope of line, $m=\tan \theta=\frac{O A}{O B}$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow m=\frac{1}{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{2}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 m-2=0 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> be three tangent lines to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>h</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></math> be three tangent lines to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>h</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
Lines y=x+2, 4y=3x+6 and 3y=4x+1 are tangents to the circle (x-h)2+(y-k)2=r2.

Centre of the circle is h,k.

Equation of bisector of lines 4y=3x+6, 3y=4x+1 is:

4x-3y+15=±3x-4y+65

⇒4x-3y+1=±3x-4y+6

Taking positive sign, we get

4x-3y+1=3x-4y+6

⇒x+y=5

Since, centre h,k lies on the bisector, therefore

h+k=5

subject: Mathematics
q: The centres of those circles which touch the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> externally and also touch the <math><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></math> - axis, lie on
question: The centres of those circles which touch the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> externally and also touch the <math><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></math> - axis, lie on
options: An ellipse which is not a circle
solution:
Given circle x2+y2-8x-8y-4=0

has centre 4, 4 and radius 6.

Let h, k be the centre of the circle which is touching the given circle externally



Then

h-42+k-42=6+k

⇒h2-8h+16+k2-8k+16=36+12k+k2

⇒h2-8h-20k-4=0 ,

Replacing h by x and k  by y, locus is

x2-8x-20y-4=0

Which is equation of parabola.

subject: Mathematics
q: The equation of the circle passing through the point $(1,2)$ and through the points of intersection of $x^2+y^2-4 x-6 y-21=0$ and $3 x+4 y+5=0$ is given by
question: The equation of the circle passing through the point $(1,2)$ and through the points of intersection of $x^2+y^2-4 x-6 y-21=0$ and $3 x+4 y+5=0$ is given by
solution:
Point $(1,2)$ lies on the circle $x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y$ $-11=0$, because coordinates of point $(1,2)$ satisfy the equation $$ x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y-11=0 $$ Now, $x^2+y^2-4 x-6 y-21=0 \quad$...(i) $$ \begin{aligned} & x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y-11=0 \\ & 3 x+4 y+5=0 \end{aligned} $$ From (i) and (iii), $$ \begin{array}{r} x^2+\left(-\frac{3 x+5}{4}\right)^2-4 x-6\left(-\frac{3 x+5}{4}\right)-21=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad 16 x^2+9 x^2+30 x+25-64 x \\ +72 x+120-336=0 \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad 25 x^2+38 x-191=0 \\ & \text { From (ii) and (iii), } \\ & x^2+\left(-\frac{3 x+5}{4}\right)^2+2 x+2\left(-\frac{3 x+5}{4}\right)-11=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 16 x^2+9 x^2+30 x+25+32 x \end{aligned} $$ Thus we get the same equation from (ii) and (iii) as we get from equation (i) and (iii). Hence the point of intersections of (ii) and (iii) will be same as the point of intersections of(i) and (iii). Therefore the circle (ii) passing through the point of intersection of circle(i) and point $(1,2)$ also as shown in the figure.
Hence equation(ii) i.e. $x^2+y^2+2 x+2 y-11=0$ is the equation of required circle.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider ellipses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>k</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><mi>k</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>20</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math> be the circle which touches the four chords joining the end points (one on minor axis and another on major axis) of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math> is the radius of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>20</mn></munderover><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msubsup><mi>r</mi><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msubsup></mfrac></math> is
question: Consider ellipses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>k</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><mi>k</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><mn>20</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math> be the circle which touches the four chords joining the end points (one on minor axis and another on major axis) of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math> is the radius of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>k</mi></msub></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>20</mn></munderover><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msubsup><mi>r</mi><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msubsup></mfrac></math> is
solution:
Given,

Ek: kx2+k2y2=1

⇒Ek :x21k2+y21k2=1

Now equation of the chord joining the points 1k,0 & 0,1k will be,

Lk:x1k+y1k=1

⇒kx+ky-1=0

Now rk= Perpendicular distance of Lk from (0,0) we get,

rk=-1k+k2

⇒rk2=1k+k2

Now putting the value of rk2 in ∑k=1201rk2 we get,

∑k=1201rk2=∑k=120k+k2=20×212+20×21×416

=210+2870=3080

subject: Mathematics
q: If the locus of the mid-point of the line segment from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to a point on the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is equal to<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
question: If the locus of the mid-point of the line segment from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to a point on the circle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is equal to<gwmw style="display:none;"></gwmw>
solution:
Given circle x2+y2=1

General coordinate on a given circle is cosθ, sinθ. 

Given, h=cosθ+32⇒cosθ=2h-32  ...i

k=sinθ+22⇒sinθ=2k-1  ...ii

Square i & ii then add we get, 

h-322+k-12=122

⇒r=12

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the line \(x+y=1\) meet the circle \(x^2+y^2=4\) at the points A and B . If the line perpendicular to \(A B\) and passing through the mid point of the chord \(A B\) intersects the circle at \(C\) and \(D\), then the area of the quadrilateral ADBC is equal to :
question: Let the line \(x+y=1\) meet the circle \(x^2+y^2=4\) at the points A and B . If the line perpendicular to \(A B\) and passing through the mid point of the chord \(A B\) intersects the circle at \(C\) and \(D\), then the area of the quadrilateral ADBC is equal to :
solution: By solving $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y}$ with circle We get $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}(\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}) \\ & \mathrm{D}(-\sqrt{2},-\sqrt{2}) \end{aligned}$ By solving $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=1$ with circle $x^2+y^2=4$ we set $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{7}}{2}, \frac{1-\sqrt{7}}{2}\right) \\ & \& \mathrm{~B}\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{7}}{2}, \frac{1+\sqrt{7}}{2}\right) \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Area of Quadrilateral ACBD $=2 \times \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{BCD}$ $\begin{aligned} & =2 \times \frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sqrt{2} & \sqrt{2} & 1 \\ \frac{1-\sqrt{7}}{2} & \frac{1+\sqrt{7}}{2} & 1 \\ -\sqrt{2} & -\sqrt{2} & 1\end{array}\right| \\ & =2 \sqrt{14}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The line <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi></math> touches a circle at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math>. If the circle also passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math>, then its radius is
question: The line <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>y</mi></math> touches a circle at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo></math>. If the circle also passes through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>)</mo></math>, then its radius is
solution:
Equation of family of circles touches line x–y=0 at point (1,1) is
(x-1)2+(y-1)2+λx–y=0, λ∈R
If a member of this family, also passes through point (1,–3) then
1-12+-3-12+λ1--3=0⇒λ=-4
⇒  Required circle is
x-12+y-12-4x-y=0
⇒ x2+y2-6x+2y+2=0
Where radius is r=32+12-2=22

subject: Mathematics
q: The equation of the circle described on the chord <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msup><mo>⁡</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><msup><mo>⁡</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mn>6</mn></math> as the diameter is
question: The equation of the circle described on the chord <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> of the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><msup><mo>⁡</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><msup><mo>⁡</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mn>6</mn></math> as the diameter is
solution:
We know that the equation of the family of circle passing through the point of intersection of a circle S=0 and a line L=0 is S+λL=0.

Hence, the family of circle passing through points of intersection of given circle x2+y2-16=0 and chord 3x+y+5=0 is (x2+y2-16)+λ3x+y+5=0

⇒x2+y2+3λx+λy+5λ-16=0

We know that, the centre of a circle x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0 is -g, -f

Hence, centre of the circle x2+y2+3λx+λy+5λ-16=0 is -3λ2, -λ2 and since, for this circle AB is diameter, thus -3λ2, -λ2  must lie on AB i.e. 3x+y+5=0

⇒3-3λ2+-λ2 +5=0

⇒-9λ2-λ2+5=0

⇒λ=1.

Therefore, the equation of the required circle is x2+y2+3×1×x+1×y+5×1-16=0

⇒x2+y2+3x+y-11=0.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> intersect at exactly two distinct points, then
question: If the circles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> intersect at exactly two distinct points, then
solution:
Given,

S1≡x+12+y+22=r2 and S2≡x2+y2-4x-4y+4=0 or S2≡x-22+y-22=22

Now, if two circles intersect at two distinct points then r1-r2<C1C2<r1+r2.

⇒r-2<32+42<r+2

⇒r-2<5<r+2

⇒r-2<5 and r+2>5

⇒-5<r-2<5 and r>3

⇒-3<r<7 and r>3

⇒3<r<7
